channel.title: Home on Zhuofan Li
channel.description: Recent content in Home on Zhuofan Li

item.title: Research
item.description:
My research projects revolve around similar theoretical and methodological interests at the intersection of knowledge, power, organizations, networks, and computational methods:

  1. Corporate Open Science

    • Draws on text and network data from ArXiv, GitHub, and in-depth interviews with clusters of researchers and organizations, my dissertation traces how corporate participation in open science recasts the knowledge infrastructure of computer vision, a rapidly commercializing subfield of artificial intelligence that mobilizes massive human behavioral and biometric data and has potentially transformative consequences on surveillance and stratification. This research has been supported by a SBS Summer Dissertation Fellowship from the College of Social and Behavioral Sciences at the University of Arizona.

  2. Socio-Semantic Networks

    • Sociologists have recently turned to network analysis for dynamic models of occupational boundaries. Similarly, in classification systems, category-spanning identities lead to categorical change. How can we use socio-semantic network analysis and word embedding models to model this dynamic and endogenous interaction between identity-making and categorical change?

  3. Networks of Temporalities

    • (with Corey Abramson and Dan Dohan) How do misalignments between individual experiences of time and the temporal structure of the field stratify actors’ ability to activate their endowments of economic, cultural, social, and bodily capital?

  4. Text as data

    • (with Corey Abramson and Dan Dohan) Coding qualitative data from team-based, multi-site comparative ethnography is tedious and error-prone. How can we use semi-supervised machine learning and pretrained language models to scale human coding in ways that do not fetter but facilitate in-depth interpretation?

item.title: Resources
item.description:
Things I found useful and fun for computational sociologists (mostly open source).

Software

Paperpile: A browser-based reference manager that downloads PDFs and saves notes and annotations to Google Drive [website]

Obsidian: A powerful knowledge base on top of a local folder of plain text Markdown files [github]

Grobid: A machine learning library for extracting information from scholarly PDFs [github]

Data

Data is Plural: A weekly newsletter of useful/curious datasets by Jeremy Singer-Vine [website]

Awesome Public Datasets: A topic-centric list of high quality open datasets [github]

Methods

MIT 18.06: Linear Algebra by Gilbert Strang [website]

Yale MGMT 737: Applied Empirical Methods for Management PhDs by Paul Goldsmith-Pinkham [github]

Text Mining with R by Julia Silge and David Robinson [website]

Visualization

From Data to Viz: leads you to the most appropriate graph for your data, links to the code to build it, and lists common caveats you should avoid, by Yan Holtz and Conor Healy [website]

MetBrewer: Color palette package in R and Python inspired by artworks at the Met by Blake Robert Mills [github]

Presentation

Demystifying the Dissertation Proposal by Leonard Cassuto [website]

Writing Tips for PhD Students by John H. Cochrane [PDF]

The Introduction Formula by Keith Head [website]

Slides on making slides by Kieran Healy [website]

Writing Proposals for ACLS Fellowship Competitions by Christina M. Gillis [PDF]

An Essay on the Art of Reviewing by Myra Marx Ferree [PDF]
